title: SHRESTH Initiative
focus: State Health Regulatory Excellence Index and its objectives for drug regulation.
rationale: A specific government initiative for drug regulation, suitable for a standalone factual question regarding its nodal agency and purpose.
sectionExcerpt: Union Health Ministry launched State Health Regulatory Excellence Index (SHRESTH), a national initiative to benchmark and strengthens state drug regulatory systems. Proposed by the CDSCO. Aim: To drive improvements in the performance of state drug regulatory authorities across India, ensuring drug safety and quality standards are consistently met.
evidenceBullets: SHRESTH was launched by the Union Health Ministry.
It was proposed by the Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO).
It aims to benchmark and strengthen state drug regulatory systems to ensure drug safety and quality.

title: Major Types of Vaccines
focus: Classification of vaccines (Live attenuated, Inactivated, Subunit, Toxoid, Conjugate, Recombinant, DNA/RNA) and their mechanisms.
rationale: Core static science concept highly relevant for UPSC Prelims. Rich in examples and distinct biological mechanisms.
sectionExcerpt: Live attenuated vaccines: Contain weakened live pathogens from bacteria or viruses. E.g., the measles, mumps, and rubella (MMR) Vaccine. Inactivated or Dead vaccines: Uses deactivated live pathogens... Subunit or Acellular vaccines: Contain polysaccharides or proteins... Toxoid Vaccine: Uses inactivated toxins to target the toxic activity... Conjugate Vaccine: Uses two specific parts of a pathogen... Recombinant Vaccine: Small piece of the DNA... DNA/RNA Vaccine: Genetic material, either DNA or RNA, from the pathogenic bacteria or virus is introduced into the human cells.
evidenceBullets: Live attenuated vaccines use weakened live pathogens (e.g., MMR vaccine).
Inactivated vaccines use deactivated pathogens to create immunity without causing disease (e.g., Polio, Influenza).
Toxoid vaccines target the toxic activity created by bacteria rather than the bacteria itself (e.g., Diphtheria).
Conjugate vaccines use two specific parts of a pathogen to enhance immune response (e.g., ZyVac TCV for Typhoid).
Recombinant vaccines use a small piece of DNA from the pathogen (e.g., Hepatitis B, HPV).

title: AdFalciVax Vaccine and Malaria
focus: India's indigenous multi-stage malaria vaccine, its dual-stage targeting mechanism, and basic facts about Malaria.
rationale: Combines a major indigenous technological breakthrough with static disease information (Malaria), making it highly testable.
sectionExcerpt: Government of India has licensed private firms to produce its first indigenous multi-stage malaria vaccine, AdFalciVax. Dual-Stage Targeting of Plasmodium falciparum... targets two critical stages in parasite's lifecycle i.e. Pre-erythrocytic (liver) stage and Transmission (sexual) stage. Recombinant Chimeric Vaccine: A chimeric vaccine refers to vaccine that combines antigens from different stages of a parasite's life cycle into a single immunogen. Malaria mostly spreads to people through bites of some infected female Anopheles mosquitoes.
evidenceBullets: AdFalciVax is India's first indigenous multi-stage malaria vaccine.
It targets two critical stages of Plasmodium falciparum: the Pre-erythrocytic (liver) stage and the Transmission (sexual) stage.
It is a Recombinant Chimeric Vaccine, combining antigens from different life cycle stages into a single immunogen.
Malaria is caused by Plasmodium parasites and spreads through infected female Anopheles mosquitoes.
Five Plasmodium species cause malaria in humans, including P. falciparum and P. vivax.

title: Oncolytic, Personalized mRNA, and saRNA Vaccines
focus: Advanced vaccine technologies including oncolytic viruses for cancer, mRNA mechanisms, and self-amplifying mRNA (saRNA).
rationale: Emerging biotechnology topics frequently tested in UPSC Science & Tech, focusing on the mechanisms of mRNA and oncolytic treatments.
sectionExcerpt: Russia is developing oncolytic vaccine Enteromix and Personalized messenger RNA (mRNA) cancer vaccines... Oncolytic vaccine uses oncolytic viruses (OVs) to directly kill cancer cells and stimulate an anti-tumor immune response. mRNA delivers genetic material, encapsulated in lipid nanoparticles, into body to prompt protein production... saRNA vaccines work by giving the body instructions to replicate mRNA needed to create the desired antigen, potentially enhancing the immune response with lower doses compared to existing mRNA vaccines.
evidenceBullets: Oncolytic vaccines use viruses to directly kill cancer cells and stimulate an anti-tumor immune response (e.g., Enteromix).
Personalized mRNA vaccines are based on the genetic analysis of a patient's specific cancer cells.
mRNA vaccines deliver genetic material encapsulated in lipid nanoparticles to prompt protein production (antigens).
Self-amplifying mRNA (saRNA) vaccines instruct the body to replicate mRNA, allowing for lower doses compared to standard mRNA vaccines.
Gennova Biopharmaceuticals is developing a saRNA vaccine candidate against the Nipah virus.

title: WHO Pandemic Agreement
focus: The Intergovernmental Working Group meeting and the legal basis of the WHO Pandemic Agreement.
rationale: Brief but important international governance update regarding global health treaties.
sectionExcerpt: WHO member states held their first meeting of the Intergovernmental Working Group (IGWG) on the WHO Pandemic Agreement. Second international legal agreement negotiated under Article 19 of the WHO Constitution (first being the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control, 2003).
evidenceBullets: The WHO Pandemic Agreement is negotiated under Article 19 of the WHO Constitution.
It is the second international legal agreement under this article, following the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (2003).
